title: Meta Garbage Collection: Using OCaml's GC to GC Rust - Soteria
excerpt: Tracking Rust's aliasing model can be quadratically expensive if done naively. Learn how we fixed this in Soteria Rust by doing meta garbage collection.
content_html:
Back to Blog

Tracking Rust's aliasing model can be quadratically expensive if done naively. Learn how we fixed this in Soteria Rust by doing meta garbage collection.

Soteria Rust is a symbolic execution tool for verifying Rust programs; it explores every execution path, catching undefined behaviour, aliasing errors, and more. We recently noticed something odd: a simple loop that increments a variable N times was taking quadratic time in N!

The culprit was our implementation of Tree Borrows , Rust’s latest and most advanced aliasing model. The fix ended up being surprisingly simple: because Soteria is written in OCaml, we can delegate the garbage collection of Tree Borrows’ state to OCaml’s garbage collector. In about 40 lines of code, we went from quadratic to linear time, with up to a 10x speedup!

Let’s look into how we found the issue, and how we fixed it.

Let’s look at the example that made us notice the problem: a simple benchmark in which we take a mutable reference r, read it once (into _old), reset it to zero, and then increment it N times in a loop, asserting that the result is indeed N.

fn loop_incr<const N: u32>(r: &mut u32) {
	let _old = *r;
	*r = 0;
	for _ in 0..N {
		*r += 1;
	}
	assert_eq!(*r, N);
}

Let’s run this with a few different Ns and measure the time:

Quadratic growth, for a linear increment… Hm. Looking at the execution time for N=1000, of the 3.02 seconds, 2.62 seconds (87%) are spent in our Tree Borrows implementation.

Tree Borrows was one of the first things I implemented in Soteria Rust, in ancient times (April 2025). It is designed to give rules for aliasing in unsafe Rust, where the borrow checker cannot help you, providing rules one must follow when manipulating raw pointers. This in turn allows for more optimisations in the compiler, at the cost of more ways to trigger undefined behaviour  (UB).

The important thing to know about Tree Borrows here is that it tracks the relationship between all references (and pointers) taken to a location in memory with a tree. Every reborrow (deriving a reference from another, e.g. with a field access) induces a new node in the tree, and every access to memory (each read or write) requires updating the state of all nodes in the tree.

That state is the heart of the model: each reference is in one of five states, and each access moves it along a state machine. The state machine has changed quite a bit since the original paper, but for the sake of presentation let’s look at the original one:

For instance, &mut T starts in the Reserved state, and &T starts in the Frozen state. The arrows between states are transitions that happen on accesses to memory; if a reference’s state ever reaches the ↯ on the right, it means that the program has UB, and has violated Rust’s aliasing rules. The symbols by the arrows tell us under what condition we can transition from one state to another. R is on reads, W is on writes, and ↓ is for local accesses while ↑ is for foreign accesses.

Because we track references in a tree, an access is local if it happens on that reference, or on a reference that was reborrowed from it (so a child node). An access is foreign otherwise. Looking at the state machine, the only way to reach UB (↯) is thus through a local access (↓); remember this for later.

Adding some debugging to the Tree Borrows access function, we can see that by the end of the loop there is a tree that has 9,010 nodes, and that is being accessed 10,012 times. However, the loop only accesses r: &mut u32 a constant number of times per iteration, so what’s happening?

An important consideration when doing code analysis is that we cannot enable optimisations, because they may rely on the absence of UB, and may erase it. Our tool wants to be able to detect UB, so that’s a no-go. Function inlining? Hides UB. Unused variable elimination? Hides UB. Statement reordering? Hides UB. We have to work with unoptimised code, which is often much less efficient, and lengthier, than optimised code.

To illustrate, this is the code generated by the Rust compiler  for this program, with the optimisation level set to 1:

fn loop_incr<const N: u32>(r: &mut u32) {
	let _old = *r;
	*r = 0;
	for _ in 0..N {
		*r += 1;
	}
	assert_eq!(*r, N);
}

pub fn main() {
	loop_incr::<1000>(&mut 0);
}
example::main::h98dd2dc58b240508:
	ret

The compiler is clever enough to show that this function is a no-op (the assertion never fails), and completely erases it! However, this is only true because the Rust type system promises that the mutable reference is non-null, well-aligned, and points to initialised and writeable memory.

So optimisations are out of the question. Let’s instead look at the MIR without optimisations , Rust’s intermediate language that Soteria Rust runs on. We can see the <Range as Iterator>  calls that the for-loop desugars into (lines 6 and 11 below). These supposedly always get inlined (this is Rust’s zero-cost abstraction promise!), but without optimisations they persist.

1 
fn loop_incr(_1: &mut u32) -> () {
2 
	bb0: {
3 
		_2 = copy (*_1);
4 
		(*_1) = const 0_u32;
5 
		_4 = std::ops::Range::<u32> { start: const 0_u32, end: const N };
6 
		_3 = <std::ops::Range<u32> as IntoIterator>::into_iter(move _4) -> [return: bb2, unwind continue];
7 
	}
8
9 
	bb2: {
10 
		_7 = &mut _3;
11 
		_6 = <std::ops::Range<u32> as Iterator>::next(copy _7) -> [return: bb3, unwind continue];
12 
	}
13
14 
	...
15 
}

This gives us a hint as to what is actually happening: this overgrown tree isn’t caused by r, but by the Range object being iterated over.

On each iteration, the tree grows by A nodes, and is accessed B times. This means in total there are roughly A * B * N² node accesses, which is quadratic in N! If you want the exact numbers, expand the details below; it turns out the 9,010 nodes and 10,012 accesses we measured earlier are unfortunately exactly what we expect to see.

The Boring Details and Maths

New nodes are created on reborrows, which happen implicitly in 1. function calls, 2. before method calls on the receiver (this is autoref ), and 3. on field accesses. If we dig deeper, we find the following reborrows in the stack:

  • before the call to Iterator::next (see MIR above) – that’s 1
  • that method call (case 2 in the list above) – 2
  • that same function call (case 1) – 3
  • the method call to spec_next inside Iterator::next  (case 2) – 4
  • the same function call (case 1) – 5
  • spec_next itself then reborrows self.start and self.end  (case 3) – 7
  • … and then calls <u32 as PartialOrd>::lt, which reborrows both again (case 1) – 9!

Every function call to <Range as Iterator>::next thus adds 9 nodes to the tree at the range’s location, which is called N + 1 times. Adding the root node to the tree, this gives us 9 * (N + 1) + 1 = 9N + 10 nodes by the end of the function, which matches the 9,010 nodes we saw for N=1000.

Looking at the code, we also see:

  • the initial borrow of the Range accesses the tree twice, as it has two fields – so 2 tree accesses total
  • each call to next does 10 accesses to the tree when it returns Some(_), and 8 otherwise – 2 + 10N + 8, for the N iterations and the final None return
  • the tree gets accessed two last times when the Range goes out of scope – 2 + 10N + 8 + 2 = 10N + 12

For N=1000, this gives us 10,012 accesses to the tree, which matches what we saw earlier too!

This explains the quadratic growth. The tree grows by A=9 nodes per iteration, and is accessed B=10 times per iteration. So for N iterations, we have 1/2 * 9 * 10 * N² = 45N² node accesses, which is quadratic in N (the 1/2 is because the tree grows linearly, so it is half-grown on average.)

So we should track these 9,010 nodes, but that seems like a lot of work. Can we avoid this? Luckily, the answer is yes! Sort of. In the best cases. And the justification is back in the state machine of Tree Borrows.

If we look more closely at the structure of the tree in our code example, we have something that looks like this, which matches the calculations we did earlier:

The important thing to notice is that each branch created by each iteration is independent; those references are created from the root, which is the variable storing the Range object.

As we saw earlier, the only way for a node to reach UB is a local access. But once an iteration is done, its branch is unreachable: no live references point to it, so nothing can perform a local access on it. Those nodes can thus never contribute to UB, and we can safely drop them from the tree once we know they aren’t reachable!

Extra soundness justifications

For those familiar with Tree Borrows, I brushed over some details here. So here are the last few justifications to show this is sound!

  • UB can actually be reached through foreign accesses when a reference is protected, which happens on function entry (it is unprotected at function exit). Garbage collecting a protected node could thus cause us to miss UB!

    Luckily for us, our interpreter keeps these references in a variable throughout the scope of the function to be able to release the protectors. This means we keep a strong reference to them, and they can’t be GCed.

  • What if some intermediate node gets GCed, but its children are still reachable? Then a local access would also be possible, and we would miss UB!

    Fortunately, due to the way the state machine is structured and reborrows work, we know a child is always at least as close to UB as its parent. Any UB the parent would cause, the child would cause too. Getting rid of the parent is thus safe, as it can’t cause more UB than the child.

    The way we organise the data structure to enable intermediate nodes to be GCed is that the tree is represented as a weak map from nodes to a set of weak nodes, which are all of that node’s ancestors, not just its parent. If an intermediate node is GCed, all its descendants lose it as an ancestor, but they still hold every other ancestor directly, so local/foreign relationships remain intact.

  • What about symbolic execution? Couldn’t this GC mechanism erase references that aren’t reachable in one execution branch from another branch? This system introduces mutability to state that is shared across parallel execution branches!

    Again, this is naturally avoided by how our interpreter works. We always hold references to the state before a branching point, so if the Tree Borrows node is reachable in the other execution branch, that reference to the state would stop it from being GCed in a branch for which it is unreachable.

    This is technically suboptimal, as it means some branches may need to keep track of references they don’t need, but it is sound and in practice we haven’t seen any issues with this.

A solution would be to implement a garbage collector in our interpreter, which traverses the heap we’re tracking and looks for which tree nodes are still reachable, clearing all the nodes that aren’t. This is however costly, as the heap can grow very large. Traversing it entirely, keeping track of encountered nodes, and then clearing those nodes is a lot of work.

Luckily for us, Soteria Rust is written in OCaml, which comes with its own garbage collector. What if we just re-used that?

Doing this is surprisingly easy! We need to ensure two things:

  1. The OCaml object for a node is held strongly wherever its Rust reference is still in use, so that a node whose reference is live can never be collected. This is already the case.
  2. Our implementation of Tree Borrows holds its nodes weakly: the GC should not consider nodes that are in the tree as impeding garbage collection, since we want them to be collected and removed. To do this, we use a mix of Weak sets  and Ephemeron , two data structures provided by OCaml that hold weak references to their elements.

That’s it! Let’s run it again.

The execution time is now… slower? And the line chart is escaping the graph.

What is happening is that the OCaml garbage collector  runs only periodically, rather than constantly. In our case, Soteria Rust doesn’t allocate much memory, so the garbage collector doesn’t run often. In this example, the garbage collector seems to never run, and we instead have to pay the cost of weak references, which are a bit more expensive to access than strong ones.

Let’s take matters into our own hands. We can’t run the GC constantly, as that would be too costly. We also want to make sure we don’t invoke it when it’s unlikely to be worth it; reborrowing a reference when the tree is small is not a problem.

Furthermore, the OCaml GC is done in two phases: a minor collection, which is fast and collects only the “young” values, and a major collection, which is slower and collects all values. The minor collection is not enough for us, as it will not collect the nodes in the tree that are no longer reachable, so we must force major collections instead, with Gc.major () .

We will run the GC when the tree exceeds size T, a separate parameter for us to play with. We also need to be cautious of cases where the tree grows past size T but the GC doesn’t free any nodes (because all references are held): we don’t want all subsequent accesses to be slowed down by a GC run. Instead, we’ll implement a “backoff” mechanism, where the GC failing to free any nodes will set the next T to 2T.

Let’s run this again; first with T=10, which is the theoretically optimal value for this example, given at most 10 nodes are reachable at any time. We also try it with T=32, 64, 128, 256, 512 to see how it behaves with less frequent GC runs.

Fantastic! The GC is now running, and we have a linear execution time again. Unsurprisingly, T=10 is actually far from optimal, as doing a GC run every 10 nodes is too costly. The best value seems to be T=256, which strikes a good balance between keeping the tree relatively small, and not running the GC too often. For N=1000, the execution time is now 0.54 seconds, which is a 5.6x speedup over the original 3.02 seconds. At N=2000, the speedup is 10.6x! Most importantly, our execution time is now linear in N, rather than quadratic.

We can also look at the improvement directly, by measuring the size of the Range’s tree after each of the 10,012 accesses. Without collection, the tree only ever grows, ending at the 9,010 nodes we predicted earlier. With T=256, we get a sawtooth pattern, growing until the threshold T is reached, where the GC runs and most nodes are collected.

Running this change across other benchmarks, we get mostly positive results; some benchmarks end up being slower, due to the cost of using weak references rather than just having straightforward strong references, but most benchmarks are faster. Importantly, this change avoids quadratic growth in relatively common patterns, so it’s a win!1

An example of performance improvement is on the code below. This snippet was taken from Tokio’s initialisation code , where we were facing some performance issues. We bumped the size of the array from the original 64 to 1024, to stress test it. This benchmark used to take 14.8 seconds; for comparison, a similar (unnamed) tool in this domain takes 15.4 seconds, without tracking Tree Borrows. After this change, it takes 1.7 seconds: an 8.7x speedup!

let arr: [LinkedList<u32>; 1024] = std::array::from_fn(|_| LinkedList {
	head: None,
	tail: None,
	_marker: PhantomData,
});

Varying the size of the array, we get the following results:

Doing garbage collection of Tree Borrows trees is not a new idea; Miri already does it , along with some other tricks  to improve performance. However, in Miri the GC must be implemented manually, since Rust is the host language. Using OCaml enables us to do it much more easily; this change was only about 40 lines of code, and is a neat form of meta garbage collection.

One of the strengths of Soteria Rust, and how we architect code in Soteria, is that we fully leverage the host language. This is one of the key design decisions Sacha, our CTO, took for the Soteria library. We have a shallow OCaml embedding and don’t provide or enforce a unique intermediate language, so the interpreter is highly customisable. It’s what allows us to solve this problem in 40 lines, instead of reimplementing a garbage collector ourselves! It’s always worth knowing your host language well enough to hand it the hard parts.

If you want to learn more about Soteria Rust, check out the documentation and the getting started guide!

1. If Tree Borrows still proves to be prohibitively slow, users can disable it with --ignore-aliasing. ↩

With T=256, we get a sawtooth pattern, growing until the threshold T is reached, where the GC runs and most nodes are collected. Running this change across other benchmarks, we get mostly positive results; some benchmarks end up being slower, due to the cost of using weak references rather than just having straightforward strong references, but most benchmarks are faster. Importantly, this change avoids quadratic growth in relatively common patterns, so it’s a win!1 An example of performance improvement is on the code below. This snippet was taken from Tokio’s initialisation code , where we were facing some performance issues. We bumped the size of the array from the original 64 to 1024, to stress test it. This benchmark used to take 14.8 seconds; for comparison, a similar (unnamed) tool in this domain takes 15.4 seconds, without tracking Tree Borrows. After this change, it takes 1.7 seconds: an 8.7x speedup! let arr: [LinkedList<u32>; 1024] = std::array::from_fn(|_| LinkedList { head: None, tail: None, _marker: PhantomData, }); Varying the size of the array, we get the following results: Doing garbage collection of Tree Borrows trees is not a new idea; Miri already does it , along with some other tricks to improve performance. However, in Miri the GC must be implemented manually, since Rust is the host language. Using OCaml enables us to do it much more easily; this change was only about 40 lines of code, and is a neat form of meta garbage collection. One of the strengths of Soteria Rust, and how we architect code in Soteria, is that we fully leverage the host language. This is one of the key design decisions Sacha, our CTO, took for the Soteria library. We have a shallow OCaml embedding and don’t provide or enforce a unique intermediate language, so the interpreter is highly customisable. It’s what allows us to solve this problem in 40 lines, instead of reimplementing a garbage collector ourselves! It’s always worth knowing your host language well enough to hand it the hard parts. If you want to learn more about Soteria Rust, check out the documentation and the getting started guide! 1. If Tree Borrows still proves to be prohibitively slow, users can disable it with --ignore-aliasing. ↩ Stay updated Subscribe to get updates on new features and releases.This is a very low volume mailing list!

blocks.html_en: Tracking Rust's aliasing model can be quadratically expensive if done naively. Learn how we fixed this in Soteria Rust by doing meta garbage collection.

sentences.en: Tracking Rust's aliasing model can be quadratically expensive if done naively.
sentences.zh: 如果简单地跟踪 Rust 的别名模型，其成本可能会成倍增加。

sentences.en: Learn how we fixed this in Soteria Rust by doing meta garbage collection.
sentences.zh: 了解我们如何通过执行元垃圾回收在 Soteria Rust 中修复此问题。

blocks.html_en: Soteria Rust is a symbolic execution tool for verifying Rust programs; it explores every execution path, catching undefined behaviour, aliasing errors, and more. We recently noticed something odd: a simple loop that increments a variable N times was taking quadratic time in N!

sentences.en: Soteria Rust is a symbolic execution tool for verifying Rust programs; it explores every execution path, catching undefined behaviour, aliasing errors, and more.

sentences.en: We recently noticed something odd: a simple loop that increments a variable N times was taking quadratic time in N!
sentences.zh: 我们最近注意到一些奇怪的事情：一个将变量递增 N 次的简单循环花费了 N 的二次方时间！

blocks.html_en: The culprit was our implementation of Tree Borrows , Rust’s latest and most advanced aliasing model. The fix ended up being surprisingly simple: because Soteria is written in OCaml, we can delegate the garbage collection of Tree Borrows’ state to OCaml’s garbage collector. In about 40 lines of code, we went from quadratic to linear time, with up to a 10x speedup!

sentences.en: The culprit was our implementation of Tree Borrows , Rust’s latest and most advanced aliasing model.
sentences.zh: 罪魁祸首是我们对 Tree Borrows 的实现，这是 Rust 最新、最先进的别名模型。

sentences.en: The fix ended up being surprisingly simple: because Soteria is written in OCaml, we can delegate the garbage collection of Tree Borrows’ state to OCaml’s garbage collector.
sentences.zh: 修复结果出人意料地简单：因为 Soteria 是用 OCaml 编写的，所以我们可以将 Tree Borrows 状态的垃圾收集委托给 OCaml 的垃圾收集器。

sentences.en: In about 40 lines of code, we went from quadratic to linear time, with up to a 10x speedup!
sentences.zh: 在大约 40 行代码中，我们从二次方时间变为了线性时间，加速高达 10 倍！

blocks.html_en: Tree Borrows was one of the first things I implemented in Soteria Rust, in ancient times (April 2025). It is designed to give rules for aliasing in unsafe Rust, where the borrow checker cannot help you, providing rules one must follow when manipulating raw pointers. This in turn allows for more optimisations in the compiler, at the cost of more ways to trigger undefined behaviour  (UB).

sentences.en: This in turn allows for more optimisations in the compiler, at the cost of more ways to trigger undefined behaviour (UB).

blocks.html_en: The important thing to know about Tree Borrows here is that it tracks the relationship between all references (and pointers) taken to a location in memory with a tree. Every reborrow (deriving a reference from another, e.g. with a field access) induces a new node in the tree, and every access to memory (each read or write) requires updating the state of all nodes in the tree.

sentences.en: The important thing to know about Tree Borrows here is that it tracks the relationship between all references (and pointers) taken to a location in memory with a tree.
sentences.zh: 关于树借用，需要了解的重要一点是，它跟踪使用树访问内存中某个位置的所有引用（和指针）之间的关系。

sentences.en: Every reborrow (deriving a reference from another, e.g. with a field access) induces a new node in the tree, and every access to memory (each read or write) requires updating the state of all nodes in the tree.
sentences.zh: 每次重新借用（从另一个导出引用，例如通过字段访问）都会在树中引入一个新节点，并且每次对内存的访问（每次读取或写入）都需要更新树中所有节点的状态。

blocks.html_en: That state is the heart of the model: each reference is in one of five states, and each access moves it along a state machine. The state machine has changed quite a bit since the original paper, but for the sake of presentation let’s look at the original one:

blocks.html_en: For instance, &mut T starts in the Reserved state, and &T starts in the Frozen state. The arrows between states are transitions that happen on accesses to memory; if a reference’s state ever reaches the ↯ on the right, it means that the program has UB, and has violated Rust’s aliasing rules. The symbols by the arrows tell us under what condition we can transition from one state to another. R is on reads, W is on writes, and ↓ is for local accesses while ↑ is for foreign accesses.

blocks.html_en: Because we track references in a tree, an access is local if it happens on that reference, or on a reference that was reborrowed from it (so a child node). An access is foreign otherwise. Looking at the state machine, the only way to reach UB (↯) is thus through a local access (↓); remember this for later.

blocks.html_en: An important consideration when doing code analysis is that we cannot enable optimisations, because they may rely on the absence of UB, and may erase it. Our tool wants to be able to detect UB, so that’s a no-go. Function inlining? Hides UB. Unused variable elimination? Hides UB. Statement reordering? Hides UB. We have to work with unoptimised code, which is often much less efficient, and lengthier, than optimised code.

blocks.html_en: As we saw earlier, the only way for a node to reach UB is a local access. But once an iteration is done, its branch is unreachable: no live references point to it, so nothing can perform a local access on it. Those nodes can thus never contribute to UB, and we can safely drop them from the tree once we know they aren’t reachable!

sentences.en: As we saw earlier, the only way for a node to reach UB is a local access.

sentences.en: But once an iteration is done, its branch is unreachable: no live references point to it, so nothing can perform a local access on it.
sentences.zh: 但是一旦迭代完成，它的分支就无法访问：没有实时引用指向它，因此没有任何东西可以对其执行本地访问。

sentences.en: Those nodes can thus never contribute to UB, and we can safely drop them from the tree once we know they aren’t reachable!

blocks.html_en:
• UB can actually be reached through foreign accesses when a reference is protected, which happens on function entry (it is unprotected at function exit). Garbage collecting a protected node could thus cause us to miss UB!

    Luckily for us, our interpreter keeps these references in a variable throughout the scope of the function to be able to release the protectors. This means we keep a strong reference to them, and they can’t be GCed.

blocks.html_en:
• What if some intermediate node gets GCed, but its children are still reachable? Then a local access would also be possible, and we would miss UB!

    Fortunately, due to the way the state machine is structured and reborrows work, we know a child is always at least as close to UB as its parent. Any UB the parent would cause, the child would cause too. Getting rid of the parent is thus safe, as it can’t cause more UB than the child.

    The way we organise the data structure to enable intermediate nodes to be GCed is that the tree is represented as a weak map from nodes to a set of weak nodes, which are all of that node’s ancestors, not just its parent. If an intermediate node is GCed, all its descendants lose it as an ancestor, but they still hold every other ancestor directly, so local/foreign relationships remain intact.

sentences.zh: 那么本地访问也是可能的，我们会想念 UB 的！

blocks.html_en:
• What about symbolic execution? Couldn’t this GC mechanism erase references that aren’t reachable in one execution branch from another branch? This system introduces mutability to state that is shared across parallel execution branches!

    Again, this is naturally avoided by how our interpreter works. We always hold references to the state before a branching point, so if the Tree Borrows node is reachable in the other execution branch, that reference to the state would stop it from being GCed in a branch for which it is unreachable.

    This is technically suboptimal, as it means some branches may need to keep track of references they don’t need, but it is sound and in practice we haven’t seen any issues with this.

sentences.en: What about symbolic execution?
sentences.zh: 那么符号执行呢？

sentences.en: Couldn’t this GC mechanism erase references that aren’t reachable in one execution branch from another branch?
sentences.zh: 难道这个 GC 机制不能删除一个执行分支中无法从另一个分支到达的引用吗？

sentences.en: This system introduces mutability to state that is shared across parallel execution branches!
sentences.zh: 该系统引入了在并行执行分支之间共享的状态可变性！

sentences.zh: 同样，我们的解释器的工作方式自然可以避免这种情况。

sentences.zh: 我们始终保留对分支点之前的状态的引用，因此，如果 Tree Borrows 节点在其他执行分支中可访问，则对该状态的引用将阻止它在无法访问的分支中被 GC。

blocks.html_en: A solution would be to implement a garbage collector in our interpreter, which traverses the heap we’re tracking and looks for which tree nodes are still reachable, clearing all the nodes that aren’t. This is however costly, as the heap can grow very large. Traversing it entirely, keeping track of encountered nodes, and then clearing those nodes is a lot of work.

sentences.en: A solution would be to implement a garbage collector in our interpreter, which traverses the heap we’re tracking and looks for which tree nodes are still reachable, clearing all the nodes that aren’t.
sentences.zh: 一个解决方案是在我们的解释器中实现一个垃圾收集器，它遍历我们正在跟踪的堆并查找哪些树节点仍然可访问，并清除所有不可访问的节点。

sentences.en: This is however costly, as the heap can grow very large.
sentences.zh: 然而，这是昂贵的，因为堆可能会变得非常大。

sentences.en: Traversing it entirely, keeping track of encountered nodes, and then clearing those nodes is a lot of work.
sentences.zh: 完全遍历它，跟踪遇到的节点，然后清除这些节点是一项艰巨的工作。

blocks.html_en: Luckily for us, Soteria Rust is written in OCaml, which comes with its own garbage collector. What if we just re-used that?

sentences.en: Luckily for us, Soteria Rust is written in OCaml, which comes with its own garbage collector.
sentences.zh: 对我们来说幸运的是，Soteria Rust 是用 OCaml 编写的，它带有自己的垃圾收集器。

sentences.en: What if we just re-used that?
sentences.zh: 如果我们重新使用它会怎么样？

blocks.html_en: Doing this is surprisingly easy! We need to ensure two things:

sentences.en: Doing this is surprisingly easy!
sentences.zh: 这样做非常简单！

sentences.en: We need to ensure two things:
sentences.zh: 我们需要确保两件事：

blocks.html_en: • The OCaml object for a node is held strongly wherever its Rust reference is still in use, so that a node whose reference is live can never be collected. This is already the case.

sentences.en: The OCaml object for a node is held strongly wherever its Rust reference is still in use, so that a node whose reference is live can never be collected.
sentences.zh: 无论 Rust 引用仍在使用，节点的 OCaml 对象都会被牢固地保存，因此永远无法收集其引用处于活动状态的节点。

sentences.en: This is already the case.
sentences.zh: 情况已经如此。

blocks.html_en: • Our implementation of Tree Borrows holds its nodes weakly: the GC should not consider nodes that are in the tree as impeding garbage collection, since we want them to be collected and removed. To do this, we use a mix of Weak sets  and Ephemeron , two data structures provided by OCaml that hold weak references to their elements.

sentences.en: Our implementation of Tree Borrows holds its nodes weakly: the GC should not consider nodes that are in the tree as impeding garbage collection, since we want them to be collected and removed.
sentences.zh: 我们的 Tree Borrows 实现对其节点的控制很弱：GC 不应将树中的节点视为阻碍垃圾收集，因为我们希望收集并删除它们。

sentences.en: To do this, we use a mix of Weak sets and Ephemeron , two data structures provided by OCaml that hold weak references to their elements.
sentences.zh: 为此，我们混合使用弱集和 Ephemeron，这是 OCaml 提供的两种数据结构，它们保存对其元素的弱引用。

blocks.html_en: That’s it! Let’s run it again.

sentences.en: That’s it!
sentences.zh: 就是这样！

sentences.en: Let’s run it again.
sentences.zh: 让我们再次运行它。

blocks.html_en: The execution time is now… slower? And the line chart is escaping the graph.

sentences.en: The execution time is now…
sentences.zh: 执行时间现在是……

sentences.en: slower?
sentences.zh: 慢点？

sentences.en: And the line chart is escaping the graph.
sentences.zh: 折线图正在脱离图形。

blocks.html_en: What is happening is that the OCaml garbage collector  runs only periodically, rather than constantly. In our case, Soteria Rust doesn’t allocate much memory, so the garbage collector doesn’t run often. In this example, the garbage collector seems to never run, and we instead have to pay the cost of weak references, which are a bit more expensive to access than strong ones.

sentences.en: What is happening is that the OCaml garbage collector runs only periodically, rather than constantly.
sentences.zh: 发生的情况是 OCaml 垃圾收集器仅定期运行，而不是持续运行。

sentences.en: In our case, Soteria Rust doesn’t allocate much memory, so the garbage collector doesn’t run often.
sentences.zh: 在我们的例子中，Soteria Rust 不会分配太多内存，因此垃圾收集器不会经常运行。

sentences.en: In this example, the garbage collector seems to never run, and we instead have to pay the cost of weak references, which are a bit more expensive to access than strong ones.
sentences.zh: 在这个例子中，垃圾收集器似乎永远不会运行，我们反而必须付出弱引用的成本，弱引用的访问成本比强引用要高一些。

blocks.html_en: Let’s take matters into our own hands. We can’t run the GC constantly, as that would be too costly. We also want to make sure we don’t invoke it when it’s unlikely to be worth it; reborrowing a reference when the tree is small is not a problem.

sentences.en: Let’s take matters into our own hands.
sentences.zh: 让我们把事情掌握在自己手中。

sentences.en: We can’t run the GC constantly, as that would be too costly.
sentences.zh: 我们不能持续运行 GC，因为那样成本太高。

sentences.en: We also want to make sure we don’t invoke it when it’s unlikely to be worth it; reborrowing a reference when the tree is small is not a problem.
sentences.zh: 我们还想确保当它不太值得时我们不会调用它；当树很小时重新借用引用不是问题。

blocks.html_en: Furthermore, the OCaml GC is done in two phases: a minor collection, which is fast and collects only the “young” values, and a major collection, which is slower and collects all values. The minor collection is not enough for us, as it will not collect the nodes in the tree that are no longer reachable, so we must force major collections instead, with Gc.major () .

sentences.en: Furthermore, the OCaml GC is done in two phases: a minor collection, which is fast and collects only the “young” values, and a major collection, which is slower and collects all values.
sentences.zh: 此外，OCaml GC 分两个阶段完成：次要收集，速度较快，仅收集“年轻”值；主要收集，速度较慢，收集所有值。

sentences.en: The minor collection is not enough for us, as it will not collect the nodes in the tree that are no longer reachable, so we must force major collections instead, with Gc.major () .
sentences.zh: 次要收集对我们来说还不够，因为它不会收集树中不再可达的节点，因此我们必须使用 Gc.major () 强制进行主要收集。

blocks.html_en: We will run the GC when the tree exceeds size T, a separate parameter for us to play with. We also need to be cautious of cases where the tree grows past size T but the GC doesn’t free any nodes (because all references are held): we don’t want all subsequent accesses to be slowed down by a GC run. Instead, we’ll implement a “backoff” mechanism, where the GC failing to free any nodes will set the next T to 2T.

sentences.en: We will run the GC when the tree exceeds size T, a separate parameter for us to play with.
sentences.zh: 当树超过大小 T（一个供我们使用的单独参数）时，我们将运行 GC。

sentences.en: We also need to be cautious of cases where the tree grows past size T but the GC doesn’t free any nodes (because all references are held): we don’t want all subsequent accesses to be slowed down by a GC run.
sentences.zh: 我们还需要警惕树增长超过大小 T 但 GC 不释放任何节点的情况（因为所有引用都被保留）：我们不希望所有后续访问都因 GC 运行而减慢。

sentences.en: Instead, we’ll implement a “backoff” mechanism, where the GC failing to free any nodes will set the next T to 2T.
sentences.zh: 相反，我们将实现一种“退避”机制，其中 GC 未能释放任何节点会将下一个 T 设置为 2T。

blocks.html_en: Let’s run this again; first with T=10, which is the theoretically optimal value for this example, given at most 10 nodes are reachable at any time. We also try it with T=32, 64, 128, 256, 512 to see how it behaves with less frequent GC runs.

sentences.en: Let’s run this again; first with T=10, which is the theoretically optimal value for this example, given at most 10 nodes are reachable at any time.
sentences.zh: 让我们再运行一​​次；首先使用 T=10，这是本示例的理论上最佳值，假设任何时间最多有 10 个节点可达。

content_html_zh:
Back to Blog

如果简单地跟踪 Rust 的别名模型，其成本可能会成倍增加。 了解我们如何通过执行元垃圾回收在 Soteria Rust 中修复此问题。

Soteria Rust 是一个用于验证 Rust 程序的符号执行工具；它探索每个执行路径，捕获未定义的行为、别名错误等。 我们最近注意到一些奇怪的事情：一个将变量递增 N 次的简单循环花费了 N 的二次方时间！

罪魁祸首是我们对 Tree Borrows 的实现，这是 Rust 最新、最先进的别名模型。 修复结果出人意料地简单：因为 Soteria 是用 OCaml 编写的，所以我们可以将 Tree Borrows 状态的垃圾收集委托给 OCaml 的垃圾收集器。 在大约 40 行代码中，我们从二次方时间变为了线性时间，加速高达 10 倍！

让我们看看如何发现问题以及如何解决它。

让我们看一下让我们注意到问题的示例：一个简单的基准测试，其中我们采用可变引用 r，将其读取一次（写入 _old），将其重置为零，然后在循环中将其递增 N 次，断言结果确实是 N。

fn loop_incr<const N: u32>(r: &mut u32) {
	let _old = *r;
	*r = 0;
	for _ in 0..N {
		*r += 1;
	}
	assert_eq!(*r, N);
}

让我们用几个不同的 N 来运行它并测量时间：

二次增长，线性增量…… 嗯。 查看 N=1000 的执行时间，在 3.02 秒中，2.62 秒 (87%) 用于我们的 Tree Borrows 实现。

Tree Borrows 是我在古代（2025 年 4 月）在 Soteria Rust 中实现的第一批东西之一。 它旨在为不安全的 Rust 中的别名提供规则，借用检查器无法帮助您，提供操作原始指针时必须遵循的规则。 这反过来又允许编译器进行更多优化，但代价是有更多方法触发未定义行为 (UB)。

关于树借用，需要了解的重要一点是，它跟踪使用树访问内存中某个位置的所有引用（和指针）之间的关系。 每次重新借用（从另一个导出引用，例如通过字段访问）都会在树中引入一个新节点，并且每次对内存的访问（每次读取或写入）都需要更新树中所有节点的状态。

该状态是模型的核心：每个引用都处于五种状态之一，并且每次访问都会沿着状态机移动它。 自原始论文以来，状态机已经发生了很大的变化，但为了演示起见，让我们看一下原始论文：

例如，&mut T 在保留状态下启动，&T 在冻结状态下启动。 状态之间的箭头是在访问内存时发生的转换；如果引用的状态达到右侧的↯，则意味着该程序具有 UB，并且违反了 Rust 的别名规则。 箭头符号告诉我们在什么条件下可以从一种状态转换到另一种状态。 R 表示读取，W 表示写入，↓ 表示本地访问，↑ 表示外部访问。

因为我们跟踪树中的引用，所以如果访问发生在该引用上，或者发生在从中重新借用的引用（因此是子节点）上，则访问是本地的。 否则访问是外国的。 从状态机来看，到达 UB (↯) 的唯一方法是通过本地访问 (↓)；稍后记住这一点。

对 Tree Borrows 访问函数添加一些调试，我们可以看到，到循环结束时，有一棵树有 9,010 个节点，并且被访问了 10,012 次。 然而，循环每次迭代仅访问 r: &mut u32 固定次数，所以发生了什么？

进行代码分析时的一个重要考虑因素是我们无法启用优化，因为它们可能依赖于 UB 的缺失，并且可能会删除它。 我们的工具希望能够检测 UB，所以这是不行的。 函数内联？ 隐藏 UB。 未使用的变量消除？ 隐藏 UB。 语句重新排序？ 隐藏 UB。 我们必须使用未优化的代码，与优化的代码相比，它们的效率通常要低得多，而且更长。

为了说明这一点，这是 Rust 编译器为此程序生成的代码，优化级别设置为 1：

fn loop_incr<const N: u32>(r: &mut u32) {
	let _old = *r;
	*r = 0;
	for _ in 0..N {
		*r += 1;
	}
	assert_eq!(*r, N);
}

pub fn main() {
	loop_incr::<1000>(&mut 0);
}
example::main::h98dd2dc58b240508:
	ret

编译器足够聪明，可以表明该函数是无操作的（断言永远不会失败），并完全删除它！ 然而，这只是正确的，因为 Rust 类型系统承诺可变引用是非空的、对齐良好的，并且指向已初始化且可写的内存。

所以优化是不可能的。 让我们看看没有优化的 MIR，这是 Soteria Rust 运行的 Rust 中间语言。 我们可以看到 <Range as Iterator> 调用 for 循环脱糖（下面第 6 行和第 11 行）。 据推测，这些总是会被内联（这是 Rust 的零成本抽象承诺！），但如果没有优化，它们就会持续存在。

1 fn Loop_incr(_1: &mut u32) -> () {
2 
	bb0: {
3 
		_2 = copy (*_1);
4 (*_1) = const 0_u32;
5 _4 = std::ops::Range::<u32> { 开始：const 0_u32，结束：const N }；
6 _3 = <std::ops::Range<u32> as IntoIterator>::into_iter(move _4) -> [返回：bb2，展开继续];
7 
	}
8
9 
	bb2: {
10 
		_7 = &mut _3;
11 _6 = <std::ops::Range<u32> as Iterator>::next(copy _7) -> [return: bb3, unwind continue];
12 
	}
13
14 
	...
15 
}

这给了我们一个关于实际发生的事情的提示：这棵过度生长的树不是由 r 引起的，而是由迭代的 Range 对象引起的。

在每次迭代中，树会增长 A 个节点，并被访问 B 次。 这意味着总共大约有 A * B * N² 节点访问，这是 N 的二次方！ 如果您想要确切的数字，请展开以下详细信息；不幸的是，我们之前测量的 9,010 个节点和 10,012 个访问正是我们所期望看到的。

The Boring Details and Maths

新节点是在重新借用时创建的，这在 1 中隐式发生。 函数调用，2. before 方法调用接收者（这是 autoref ），以及 3. 关于现场访问。 如果我们深入挖掘，我们会在堆栈中找到以下重借：

  • 在调用 Iterator::next 之前（参见上面的 MIR）——即 1
  • 该方法调用（上面列表中的情况 2） – 2
  • 相同的函数调用（情况 1）– 3
  • Iterator::next 中对 spec_next 的方法调用（案例 2） – 4
  • 相同的函数调用（情况 1） – 5
  • 然后spec_next本身重新借用self.start和self.end（情况3） – 7
  • … 然后调用 <u32 as PartialOrd>::lt，这会再次重新借用两者（情况 1） – 9！

每次对 <Range as Iterator>::next 的函数调用都会在范围位置的树中添加 9 个节点，这会被调用 N + 1 次。 将根节点添加到树中，到函数结束时我们得到 9 * (N + 1) + 1 = 9N + 10 个节点，这与我们在 N=1000 时看到的 9,010 个节点相匹配。

查看代码，我们还看到：

  • Range 的初始借用访问树两次，因为它有两个字段 - 所以总共 2 次树访问
  • 每次调用 next 在返回 Some(_) 时都会对树进行 10 次访问，否则进行 8 次访问 – 2 + 10N + 8，对于 N 次迭代和最终 None 返回
  • 当 Range 超出范围时，树会被最后访问两次 – 2 + 10N + 8 + 2 = 10N + 12

对于 N=1000，这为我们提供了 10,012 次对树的访问，这也与我们之前看到的相符！

这解释了二次增长。 每次迭代树会增长 A=9 个节点，每次迭代会被访问 B=10 次。 因此，对于 N 次迭代，我们有 1/2 * 9 * 10 * N² = 45N² 节点访问，这是 N 的二次方（1/2 是因为树是线性增长的，所以平均是半增长的。）

所以我们应该跟踪这 9,010 个节点，但这看起来工作量很大。 我们能避免这种情况吗？ 幸运的是，答案是肯定的！ 有点像。 在最好的情况下。 理由又回到了 Tree Borrows 的状态机。

如果我们更仔细地观察代码示例中的树结构，我们会得到如下所示的内容，它与我们之前所做的计算相匹配：

需要注意的重要一点是，每次迭代创建的每个分支都是独立的；这些引用是从根创建的，根是存储 Range 对象的变量。

正如我们之前看到的，节点到达 UB 的唯一途径是本地访问。 但是一旦迭代完成，它的分支就无法访问：没有实时引用指向它，因此没有任何东西可以对其执行本地访问。 因此，这些节点永远不会对 UB 做出贡献，一旦我们知道它们不可访问，我们就可以安全地将它们从树中删除！

Extra soundness justifications

对于那些熟悉 Tree Borrows 的人来说，我在这里回顾了一些细节。 所以这里有最后几个理由来证明这是合理的！

  • 当引用受保护时，实际上可以通过外部访问到达 UB，这发生在函数入口处（在函数出口处不受保护）。 因此，收集受保护节点的垃圾可能会导致我们错过 UB！ 幸运的是，我们的解释器将这些引用保留在整个函数范围内的变量中，以便能够释放保护器。 这意味着我们对它们保持强引用，并且它们不能被GC。
  • 如果某个中间节点被 GC 了，但其子节点仍然可达，该怎么办？ 那么本地访问也是可能的，我们会想念 UB 的！ 幸运的是，由于状态机的结构和重新借用工作的方式，我们知道子级总是至少与其父级一样接近 UB。 父母造成的任何 UB，孩子也会造成。 因此，摆脱父母是安全的，因为它不会导致比孩子更多的 UB。 我们组织数据结构以使中间节点能够被 GC 的方式是将树表示为从节点到一组弱节点的弱映射，这些弱节点是该节点的所有祖先，而不仅仅是其父节点。 如果一个中间节点被GC，它的所有后代都会失去它作为祖先的地位，但它们仍然直接保留所有其他祖先，因此本地/外部关系保持不变。
  • 那么符号执行呢？ 难道这个 GC 机制不能删除一个执行分支中无法从另一个分支到达的引用吗？ 该系统引入了在并行执行分支之间共享的状态可变性！ 同样，我们的解释器的工作方式自然可以避免这种情况。 我们始终保留对分支点之前的状态的引用，因此，如果 Tree Borrows 节点在其他执行分支中可访问，则对该状态的引用将阻止它在无法访问的分支中被 GC。 从技术上讲，这不是最理想的，因为这意味着某些分支可能需要跟踪它们不需要的引用，但这是合理的，并且在实践中我们没有看到任何问题。

一个解决方案是在我们的解释器中实现一个垃圾收集器，它遍历我们正在跟踪的堆并查找哪些树节点仍然可访问，并清除所有不可访问的节点。 然而，这是昂贵的，因为堆可能会变得非常大。 完全遍历它，跟踪遇到的节点，然后清除这些节点是一项艰巨的工作。

对我们来说幸运的是，Soteria Rust 是用 OCaml 编写的，它带有自己的垃圾收集器。 如果我们重新使用它会怎么样？

这样做非常简单！ 我们需要确保两件事：

  1. 无论 Rust 引用仍在使用，节点的 OCaml 对象都会被牢固地保存，因此永远无法收集其引用处于活动状态的节点。 情况已经如此。
  2. 我们的 Tree Borrows 实现对其节点的控制很弱：GC 不应将树中的节点视为阻碍垃圾收集，因为我们希望收集并删除它们。 为此，我们混合使用弱集和 Ephemeron，这是 OCaml 提供的两种数据结构，它们保存对其元素的弱引用。

就是这样！ 让我们再次运行它。

执行时间现在是…… 慢点？ 折线图正在脱离图形。

发生的情况是 OCaml 垃圾收集器仅定期运行，而不是持续运行。 在我们的例子中，Soteria Rust 不会分配太多内存，因此垃圾收集器不会经常运行。 在这个例子中，垃圾收集器似乎永远不会运行，我们反而必须付出弱引用的成本，弱引用的访问成本比强引用要高一些。

让我们把事情掌握在自己手中。 我们不能持续运行 GC，因为那样成本太高。 我们还想确保当它不太值得时我们不会调用它；当树很小时重新借用引用不是问题。

此外，OCaml GC 分两个阶段完成：次要收集，速度较快，仅收集“年轻”值；主要收集，速度较慢，收集所有值。 次要收集对我们来说还不够，因为它不会收集树中不再可达的节点，因此我们必须使用 Gc.major () 强制进行主要收集。

当树超过大小 T（一个供我们使用的单独参数）时，我们将运行 GC。 我们还需要警惕树增长超过大小 T 但 GC 不释放任何节点的情况（因为所有引用都被保留）：我们不希望所有后续访问都因 GC 运行而减慢。 相反，我们将实现一种“退避”机制，其中 GC 未能释放任何节点会将下一个 T 设置为 2T。

让我们再运行一​​次；首先使用 T=10，这是本示例的理论上最佳值，假设任何时间最多有 10 个节点可达。 我们还尝试使用 T=32、64、128、256、512，看看它在 GC 运行频率较低时的表现如何。

极好的！ GC 现在正在运行，我们再次获得线性执行时间。 毫不奇怪，T=10 实际上远非最佳，因为每 10 个节点运行一次 GC 成本太高。 最佳值似乎是 T=256，它在保持树相对较小和不经常运行 GC 之间取得了良好的平衡。 对于 N=1000，执行时间现在为 0.54 秒，比原来的 3.02 秒加速了 5.6 倍。 当 N=2000 时，加速比为 10.6 倍！ 最重要的是，我们的执行时间现在是 N 的线性，而不是二次。

我们还可以通过在每次 10,012 次访问后测量 Range 树的大小来直接查看改进情况。 如果没有收集，树只会不断增长，最终达到我们之前预测的 9,010 个节点。 当 T=256 时，我们得到一个锯齿模式，不断增长直到达到阈值 T，此时 GC 运行并且大多数节点被收集。

在其他基准测试中运行此更改，我们得到的结果大多是积极的；由于使用弱引用而不是直接使用强引用的成本，一些基准测试最终会变慢，但大多数基准测试速度更快。 重要的是，这一变化避免了相对常见模式中的二次增长，因此这是一场胜利！1

下面的代码是性能改进的示例。 该片段取自 Tokio 的初始化代码，我们在其中面临一些性能问题。 我们将数组的大小从原来的 64 个增加到 1024 个，以对其进行压力测试。 该基准测试过去需要 14.8 秒；为了进行比较，该领域中的类似（未命名）工具需要 15.4 秒，而不跟踪树借用。 进行此更改后，需要 1.7 秒：加速 8.7 倍！

let arr: [LinkedList<u32>; 1024] = std::array::from_fn(|_| LinkedList {
	head: None,
	tail: None,
	_marker: PhantomData,
});

对 Tree Borrows 树进行垃圾收集并不是一个新想法； Miri 已经做到了这一点，以及其他一些提高性能的技巧。 然而，在 Miri 中，GC 必须手动实现，因为 Rust 是宿主语言。 使用 OCaml 使我们能够更轻松地做到这一点；这个改变只有大约 40 行代码，并且是元垃圾收集的一种简洁形式。

Soteria Rust 的优势之一以及我们如何在 Soteria 中构建代码的方式是我们充分利用主机语言。 这是我们的 CTO Sacha 为 Soteria 库做出的关键设计决策之一。 我们有浅层 OCaml 嵌入，不提供或强制执行独特的中间语言，因此解释器是高度可定制的。 这使得我们能够在 40 行内解决这个问题，而不是自己重新实现垃圾收集器！ 充分了解您的宿主语言以便将困难的部分交给它总是值得的。
